pieces.title: The Permanent Deficit
pieces.standfirst: The Republican reconciliation bill is being assessed as a tax cut and a distributional event. Its more consequential effect is architectural: roughly $200 billion in new annual debt service payments that are constitutionally prior to all discretionary spending, binding every successor government without requiring anything like the supermajority a constitutional amendment would demand.

pieces.title: Trump-Xi Beijing Summit Opens Under Iran Shadow
pieces.standfirst: Trump arrived in Beijing on May 12 for a state visit whose formal agenda — tariffs, rare earths, Taiwan, AI — has been crowded by an Iran ceasefire Trump himself called 'on massive life support,' with Xi holding more diplomatic leverage on Tehran than the White House had planned for.

pieces.title: April CPI Reaccelerates to 3.8% as Energy Shock Hits Headline
pieces.standfirst: Headline CPI rose to 3.8% in April — the highest since May 2023 — driven by a 17.9% annual energy surge, but core inflation also reaccelerated to 2.8%, complicating the Fed's three-meeting hold and the White House's push for cuts.

pieces.title: Victory Day Ceasefire Expires; Putin Floats Third-Country Meeting
pieces.standfirst: The US-brokered three-day Victory Day ceasefire expired May 11 with mutual violation claims, even as the 1,000-for-1,000 prisoner swap proceeded and Putin offered, conditionally, to meet Zelenskyy in a third country once a long-term treaty is in place.

pieces.title: Starmer Loses Four Junior Ministers as Leadership Threshold Looms
pieces.standfirst: Four junior ministers resigned May 12, with at least 83 Labour MPs publicly calling for Starmer to go — within striking distance of the 81-MP threshold to trigger a formal contest — as the State Opening of Parliament proceeds on May 13.

pieces.title: NBA Semifinals: Knicks and Thunder Sweep In; Spurs Take 3-2 Lead
pieces.standfirst: Three of four conference semifinals series effectively decided: Knicks and Thunder sweep into the conference finals, Spurs lead Timberwolves 3-2 with Wembanyama dominant, and Cavs-Pistons stays level entering Game 5 on May 13.

pieces.title: Deterrence Against Attrition
pieces.standfirst: The US deterrence posture in the Taiwan Strait is built to prevent an amphibious invasion, but Beijing's lead instrument is coercive attrition — a campaign whose visible operations are commercial, against which no element of the deterrence kit is responsive.

pieces.title: Cloudflare Cuts 1,100 Jobs at Record Revenue
pieces.standfirst: Cloudflare announced its first mass layoff in sixteen years on May 7 — 1,100 jobs eliminated alongside record quarterly revenue — and explicitly attributed the cut to internal AI agent adoption. The move is the clearest disclosed case yet of AI displacement at a growing company.

pieces.title: Wall Street's AI Chip Rotation
pieces.standfirst: AMD, Intel, and Micron have outrun Nvidia by orders of magnitude year-to-date as Wall Street rotates AI-hardware capital from training GPUs to inference-era CPUs and memory. Nvidia's counter-move is a 0 billion equity-investment campaign across its own customer base.

pieces.title: Warsh Clears Cloture as Partisan Fed Confirmation Concludes
pieces.standfirst: The Senate's Monday cloture vote on Kevin Warsh's Fed nomination — 49-44 on party lines — caps the most strictly partisan confirmation process in Fed Chair history. Warsh inherits a Fed pinned between an Iran-war energy shock and a shutdown-distorted core inflation print due Tuesday.

pieces.title: Westminster Briefing — 11 May 2026: Starmer's Survival Week
pieces.standfirst: Sir Keir Starmer survives Monday without an immediate leadership challenge but with 77 Labour MPs publicly demanding his resignation timetable. The reset speech committed to British Steel nationalisation and a Europe-facing reset; gilts sold off regardless.

pieces.title: NBA Conference Semifinals — 11 May 2026
pieces.standfirst: The Knicks complete a four-game sweep of the 76ers behind a postseason three-point barrage; Donovan Mitchell ties an NBA playoff record with 39 second-half points to drag Cleveland back to 2-2 against Detroit. The Thunder were in Los Angeles overnight chasing a sweep of the Lakers.

pieces.title: The Housing Compact
pieces.standfirst: Housing affordability is not a supply problem. It is a political compact whose beneficiaries are also the cohort with the highest electoral turnout — and the supply-side consensus that now dominates the policy debate is an ideologically convenient way of avoiding that fact.

pieces.title: The Last Constraint
pieces.standfirst: The political fight over central bank independence presents itself as a quarrel over rates. It is something else — the contest over whether the last binding fiscal check on elected governments survives, after every other constraint has been allowed to lapse.

pieces.title: The Precariat Firm
pieces.standfirst: The consequential effect of AI on labour is not that it is replacing workers — it is that firms are using AI adoption as the political cover to dissolve the employment relationship itself. The displacement is from employee to contractor to agent, not from human to machine.

pieces.title: Hormuz Under Fire
pieces.standfirst: US fires on Iranian tankers as Hormuz talks stall; ceasefire holds nominally. A US Navy F/A-18 disabled two Iranian-flagged tankers attempting to breach the naval blockade. Iran has yet to respond to a 14-point MOU proposed by Washington.

pieces.title: Trade Court Strikes Down Trump's Section 122 Tariffs
pieces.standfirst: A 2-1 panel of the US Court of International Trade ruled on May 7 that the administration's 10% global import surcharge exceeds presidential authority under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 — but limited relief to three named plaintiffs, leaving the surcharge in place for all other US importers.

pieces.title: Hostage Infrastructure
pieces.standfirst: Gulf sovereign AI investment replicates the petrodollar bargain — resource wealth traded for US protection — but the collateral being offered is fixed infrastructure inside the threat zone, not liquid assets that hold their value in a crisis.

pieces.title: China's Legal Shield
pieces.standfirst: China's Ministry of Commerce invoked its Blocking Rules for the first time on 2 May, ordering domestic firms not to comply with US sanctions on five Chinese oil refineries. The legal escalation transforms a bilateral diplomatic dispute into a multilateral compliance problem for any international bank or shipper caught between Washington and Beijing.

pieces.title: Dollar Friendly Fire
pieces.standfirst: Dollar exclusion is a coercive instrument that depreciates with use: each sanctions episode demonstrates to every state precisely why holding dollar reserves is dangerous, and accelerates the construction of alternatives it was meant to forestall.

pieces.title: White House Reverses on AI Oversight
pieces.standfirst: An administration that spent its first year opposing AI oversight is now drafting an executive order requiring pre-deployment government vetting of advanced models, following the Mythos security alarm. The reversal signals that national security framing is doing what the broader AI safety debate failed to do.

pieces.title: The Ratchet
pieces.standfirst: Each year, roughly a third of US Treasury debt matures and must be refinanced. The bonds issued at the historic lows of 2020 are now rolling over at rates three to four times higher. The mechanism is arithmetic, not political — and unlike a deficit or a spending increase, it cannot be reversed by any future administration's decisions.

pieces.title: The Pharma Tariff Tier
pieces.standfirst: Trump's Section 232 pharmaceutical tariffs impose 100% on patented drug imports from 17 named companies, with a July 31 deadline. The tiered structure — 100% default, 20% for approved onshoring plans, zero for MFN holders — creates a compliance landscape almost entirely absent from mainstream coverage.

pieces.title: The Subtraction Problem
pieces.standfirst: US net migration turned negative in 2025 for the first time in decades. This is being covered as an immigration enforcement story. It is also a structural labour economics story, and the arithmetic has no substitute.

pieces.title: The Regulatory Sovereign
pieces.standfirst: Europe has constructed the world's most sophisticated regulatory architecture — the AI Act, the Digital Markets Act, the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism — and remains structurally dependent on American compute, American security guarantees, and American capital markets. At the moment when it faces its greatest strategic demands in a generation — rearmament, energy independence, industrial renewal — the EU's fiscal framework makes the capital required structurally impossible to raise. The regulatory sovereign can govern the markets of others; it cannot, by governing alone, become sovereign over the forces that will determine which societies thrive.

pieces.title: The Bluff That Held, The Logic That Didn't
pieces.standfirst: Operation Sindoor has been celebrated as proof that nuclear deterrence between India and Pakistan is robust — India struck deep into Pakistani territory, the threshold held, and the crisis was managed. The argument here is that this triumphalist reading is precisely wrong: both states have now learned through practice that limited war under nuclear shadow is manageable, and that belief, rather than the arsenals themselves, is the primary new source of instability in South Asia.

pieces.title: The Worst of Both Worlds
pieces.standfirst: Trump's economic interventionism is not a version of Chinese state capitalism or European industrial policy — it is political capitalism: a patronage system in which state power functions as an instrument of personal deal-making. The result combines the inefficiency risks of state intervention with the concentration risks of unaccountable private power, without the compensating benefits of either.

pieces.title: The Ownership Problem
pieces.standfirst: The AI productivity revolution is a capital distribution event, not primarily a labour market event. The policy debate is asking the wrong question — not how to compensate displaced labour, but who will own the productive capacity of the AI economy.

pieces.title: Knowledge Without Stakes
pieces.standfirst: What makes human knowledge trustworthy is not that it is socially produced — it is that it is answerable to consequences. As AI systems become more autonomous, the gap between the surface of accountable knowledge and the absence of its underlying structure is where the real risk lives.
